There are two most famous historical figures named **Michael Collins**. Here is a biography of each:

## Michael Collins — Irish Revolutionary Leader (1890–1922)

**Michael Collins** (Irish: *Mícheál Ó Coileáin*; 16 October 1890 – 22 August 1922) was an Irish revolutionary, soldier, and politician who was one of the leading figures in the early-20th-century struggle for Irish independence. Nicknamed **"The Big Fellow,"** he served as Director of Intelligence of the IRA, Minister for Finance, and Chairman of the Provisional Government of the Irish Free State before being assassinated during the Irish Civil War at just 31 years old.

### Early Life
Collins was born in **Woodfield, near Clonakilty, County Cork**, the youngest of eight children in a farming family. His father, Michael John Collins (1815–1897), was a farmer and member of the Irish Republican Brotherhood (IRB) who died when Michael was only six. A bright and precocious child with a fiery temper and fierce Irish patriotism, he was inspired by local nationalists including his schoolmaster Denis Lyons (an IRB member) and blacksmith James Santry.

At age 15, Collins moved to **London** (1906) to work as a clerk in the Post Office Savings Bank. He later worked as a messenger at a stockbrokerage firm and studied law at King's College London (though he did not complete his degree). Through the **London GAA** (Gaelic Athletic Association), he was introduced to the **Irish Republican Brotherhood** by fellow Cork man Sam Maguire in 1909.

### Easter Rising (1916)
Collins returned to Ireland in January 1916 and participated in the **Easter Rising**, serving as aide-de-camp and bodyguard to Joseph Plunkett at the rebellion's headquarters in Dublin's General Post Office (GPO). After the Rising's six-day failure, he was arrested and interned at **Frongoch camp** in Wales — an experience that proved invaluable for networking with republicans nationwide. He was released in December 1916 and quickly emerged as a major figure in the post-Rising independence movement, hatching plans for "next time."

### War of Independence (1919–1921)
Collins rose rapidly through the ranks of both **Sinn Féin** and the **Irish Volunteers**. In the December 1918 general election, he was elected MP for South Cork. Sinn Féin's members established the **First Dáil** in January 1919 and declared Irish independence. Collins was appointed **Minister for Finance** and organized a highly successful illegal "National Loan" that raised nearly **£400,000** to fund the Republic.

During the War of Independence, Collins held multiple roles: **Director of Intelligence of the IRA**, Director of Organisation, and Adjutant General of the Volunteers. He pioneered guerrilla warfare tactics, creating a specialist assassination unit known as **"The Squad"** to target British agents and informers. His most famous operation was the coordinated assassination of key British intelligence agents on **"Bloody Sunday"** (21 November 1920). The British offered **£10,000** for his capture — roughly £570,000 today — but he evaded them throughout the war.

### Anglo-Irish Treaty and Death
After the July 1921 ceasefire, Collins was one of five plenipotentiaries sent to London to negotiate peace. The resulting **Anglo-Irish Treaty** (December 1921) established the Irish Free State but required an oath of allegiance to the Crown. Collins viewed it as offering **"the freedom to achieve freedom"** and persuaded a majority of the Dáil to ratify it.

This split the republican movement and triggered the **Irish Civil War**. Collins became **Chairman of the Provisional Government** (January 1922) and **commander-in-chief of the National Army** (July 1922). On **22 August 1922**, he was shot and killed in an ambush by anti-Treaty forces at **Béal na Bláth, County Cork**. He was 31 years old. He is buried at **Glasnevin Cemetery**, Dublin.

His grandniece Nora Owen later served as Irish Minister for Justice, and another grandniece, Mary Banotti, served as an MEP.

---

## Michael Collins — American Astronaut (1930–2021)

**Michael Collins** (31 October 1930 – 28 April 2021) was an American astronaut best known as the **Command Module Pilot of Apollo 11** — the first mission to land humans on the Moon. While Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin walked on the lunar surface, Collins orbited above in the command module *Columbia*, earning him the nickname **"the loneliest man in history."**

### Early Life and Military Career
Collins was born in **Rome, Italy**, where his father was a U.S. Army officer stationed abroad. He graduated from the U.S. Military Academy at West Point in 1952 and joined the **U.S. Air Force**, becoming a fighter pilot and later a test pilot at Edwards Air Force Base.

### NASA Career
Selected as part of **NASA Astronaut Group 3** ("The Fourteen") in 1963, Collins first flew in space on **Gemini 10** (July 1966), conducting two spacewalks and performing the first double rendezvous in orbit.

His most historic mission was **Apollo 11** (July 1969). As Command Module Pilot, he remained in lunar orbit for over 21 hours while Armstrong and Aldrin explored the Moon's surface. He orbited the Moon 30 times, at times completely out of radio contact with both Earth and his crewmates on the far side of the Moon.

### Later Life
After leaving NASA in 1970, Collins served as **Assistant Secretary of State for Public Affairs** and later became Director of the **National Air and Space Museum** in Washington, D.C., overseeing its construction and opening in 1976. He also wrote several books, including his autobiography *Carrying the Fire* (1974), widely considered one of the finest astronaut memoirs.

He died on **28 April 2021** at age 90.

I'll research Chadwick Boseman's biography to provide you with accurate and comprehensive information.I have comprehensive information from Wikipedia. Let me compile a detailed biography.

---

# Chadwick Boseman — Biography

## Overview

**Chadwick Aaron Boseman** (November 29, 1976 – August 28, 2020) was an American actor and playwright. Over a two-decade career spanning both blockbuster and independent films, he received numerous accolades, including a Golden Globe Award, a Screen Actors Guild Award, and a Primetime Emmy Award, as well as posthumous nominations for an Academy Award and a BAFTA Award. [来源: Wikipedia – Chadwick Boseman]

---

## Early Life and Education

Chadwick Boseman was born on **November 29, 1976**, in **Anderson, South Carolina**, where he was raised. His mother, **Carolyn**, was a nurse, and his father, **Leroy**, worked at a textile factory and managed an upholstery business. As a youth, he practiced martial arts — training he continued into adulthood — and initially aspired to become an architect. DNA testing later revealed that some of his ancestors were **Jola people** from Guinea-Bissau, **Krio and Limba people** from Sierra Leone, and **Yoruba people** from Nigeria.

Boseman graduated from **T. L. Hanna High School** in 1995, where he played on the basketball team and competed in Speech and Debate (placing eighth nationally in Original Oratory). In his junior year, after a classmate was shot and killed, he wrote and staged his first play, *Crossroads*, at the school.

Though recruited to play basketball in college, Boseman chose the arts. He attended **Howard University** in Washington, D.C., graduating in **2000** with a **Bachelor of Fine Arts in directing**. At Howard, his mentors included **Al Freeman Jr.** and **Phylicia Rashad**, who helped raise funds — including from her friend **Denzel Washington** — so Boseman and classmates could attend the **British American Drama Academy's Oxford Summer Program** at Balliol College, Oxford in 1998. He also traveled to **Ghana** during college to work on preserving and celebrating African performance rituals, an experience he called "one of the most significant learning experiences of my life." He later completed additional film studies at New York City's **Digital Film Academy**.

---

## Early Career: Theater and Television (2000–2007)

Boseman began his career in Brooklyn, New York. In **2000**, he was named a **Drama League Directing Fellow**, directing productions including George C. Wolfe's *The Colored Museum* and Amiri Baraka's *Dutchman*. Between **2002 and 2009**, he served as a drama instructor in the **Schomburg Junior Scholars Program** at the Schomburg Center for Research in Black Culture in Harlem.

As a playwright and stage actor, he:
- Won an **AUDELCO Award** in 2002 for his performance in Ron Milner's *Urban Transitions*
- Performed with the **National Shakespeare Company of New York** (Romeo in *Romeo and Juliet*, Malcolm in *Macbeth*)
- Wrote and performed plays as part of the **Hip-Hop theater movement**, including *Rhyme Deferred* (co-written with Kamilah Forbes) and *Hieroglyphic Graffiti*

His best-known play, ***Deep Azure*** (2004), was commissioned by the Congo Square Theatre Company in Chicago and **nominated for a 2006 Jeff Award** for Best New Work. A daring work about police brutality — largely delivered in rhyme — it was highly praised by critics and workshopped at the **Apollo Theater**.

His first television role came in **2003** with an episode of *Third Watch*, followed by a role on the soap opera *All My Children*. Notably, **he was fired from *All My Children* after voicing concerns about racist stereotypes in the script** — the role was subsequently recast with his future *Black Panther* co-star **Michael B. Jordan**.

---

## Breakthrough: *42* and *Get on Up* (2008–2015)

Boseman moved to **Los Angeles** in 2008 to pursue film acting full-time. After smaller roles in *Lincoln Heights*, *The Express: The Ernie Davis Story* (2008), and *Persons Unknown* (2010), his **breakthrough came in 2013**.

### *42* (2013)
He was cast as baseball legend **Jackie Robinson** in the biopic ***42***. Director Brian Helgeland chose Boseman after being impressed by his bravery in auditioning with the script's most difficult scene. Boseman trained for **five months** with professional baseball coaches to replicate Robinson's mannerisms. Robinson's widow, **Rachel Robinson**, said seeing Boseman's performance was "like seeing her husband again." Critics widely praised his portrayal.

### *Get on Up* (2014)
Just a year later, Boseman transformed into the **Godfather of Soul, James Brown**, in ***Get on Up***. He did **all of his own dancing** and some singing, training **5–8 hours a day for two months** with choreographer Aakomon Jones, and receiving direction from producer **Mick Jagger** on live performance. Co-star **Dan Aykroyd**, who had known Brown personally, praised Boseman: "I did not have to squint sitting across from [him] to imagine that I was talking to [Brown]."

Critics lauded the performance. *Time*'s Richard Corliss wrote that Boseman "deserves a Pulitzer, a Nobel and instant election to the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame."

---

## Global Stardom: The Marvel Cinematic Universe (2016–2019)

### Becoming Black Panther
In **2016**, Boseman began portraying **T'Challa / Black Panther** in the MCU. He did **not audition** — instead, he had a creative discussion with Marvel about the character's vision. He insisted on using the **Xhosa language** for the character (learned from co-star John Kani) and developed a **Wakandan accent** that he maintained throughout production, on and off camera.

His first appearance in ***Captain America: Civil War*** (2016) was highly praised, with critics noting the character brought a fresh and vital perspective to the Avengers conflict.

### *Black Panther* (2018)
Directed by **Ryan Coogler**, ***Black Panther*** was a **landmark in cinema history** — the first mega-budget film with a predominantly Black cast and director, and the **first superhero film nominated for the Academy Award for Best Picture**. It became one of the highest-grossing films ever made.

Boseman was named to the **2018 Time 100** list of the world's most influential people. Critics praised his "sub-Shakespearean gravitas" and "quiet dignity," with Peter Travers writing that Boseman "digs so deep into T'Challa that you can feel his nerve endings."

### *Avengers: Infinity War* (2018) & *Avengers: Endgame* (2019)
Boseman reprised the role in both films. Each became the **highest-grossing film of its respective year**, with *Endgame* becoming the **highest-grossing film of all time** (at that point). His last physical appearance as Black Panther was at **Tony Stark's funeral** in *Endgame*.

### *Marshall* (2017)
Between Marvel films, Boseman portrayed a young **Thurgood Marshall** — years before Marshall became the first African American Supreme Court Justice. The film premiered at **Howard University**, which both Boseman and Marshall had attended.

---

## Final Role and Legacy

### *Ma Rainey's Black Bottom* (2020)
Boseman's final film role was as **Levee Green** in the Netflix adaptation of August Wilson's ***Ma Rainey's Black Bottom***, directed by George C. Wolfe (who had previously directed Boseman's early theater work). His performance was hailed as extraordinary:
- **Golden Globe Award** for Best Actor (posthumous)
- **Screen Actors Guild Award** for Best Actor (posthumous)
- **Academy Award nomination** for Best Actor (posthumous)

### *What If...?* (2021)
He posthumously voiced alternate versions of T'Challa in the Disney+ animated series ***What If...?***, earning a **posthumous Primetime Emmy Award** for Outstanding Character Voice-Over Performance.

---

## Personal Life and Death

Boseman married singer **Taylor Simone Ledward** in a private ceremony. In **2016**, he was diagnosed with **colon cancer**. He kept his illness entirely private, continuing to film and perform through chemotherapy and surgeries while the public was unaware of his condition.

**Chadwick Boseman passed away on August 28, 2020**, at his home in Los Angeles. He was **43 years old**. He was buried in his hometown of Anderson, South Carolina.

His death sent shockwaves worldwide, as even close colleagues had been unaware of his illness. Tributes poured in from across the globe — from fans, fellow actors, musicians, athletes, and political figures alike.

---

## Impact and Legacy

Chadwick Boseman's legacy extends far beyond his filmography:

- 🎭 **A classically trained artist** who moved seamlessly between theater, independent film, and global blockbusters
- 🏔️ **A trailblazer** who became the **first Black actor to headline an MCU film** and helped redefine what a superhero movie could be
- ✊ **A principled artist** who, from his earliest roles, challenged stereotypes and demanded better representation
- 💪 **A symbol of perseverance** who delivered some of his greatest performances while privately battling cancer
- 👑 **An icon to millions of Black children worldwide** who could finally see a superhero who looked like them — a king of an uncolonized African nation

As he said while accepting the SAG Award in 2019:

> *"We know what it's like to be told there isn't a screen for you to be featured on, a stage for you to be featured on. We know what it's like to be beneath and not above. And that is what we went to work with every day. We knew that we could create a world that exemplified a world we wanted to see."*

---

**Born:** November 29, 1976, Anderson, South Carolina
**Died:** August 28, 2020, Los Angeles, California (aged 43)
**Cause of death:** Colon cancer
**Alma mater:** Howard University (BFA)
**Spouse:** Taylor Simone Ledward
**Notable awards:** Golden Globe, SAG Award, Primetime Emmy (all for *Ma Rainey's Black Bottom* and *What If...?*); Academy Award nomination (posthumous)

*[综合来源: Wikipedia – Chadwick Boseman]*
Provided a comprehensive biography of Chadwick Boseman (1976–2020), the American actor and playwright. Key points: born in Anderson, SC; graduated from Howard University with a BFA in directing; breakthrough roles as Jackie Robinson in 42 (2013) and James Brown in Get on Up (2014); achieved global fame as T'Challa/Black Panther in the MCU (2016–2019); died of colon cancer at age 43 on August 28, 2020; posthumously won Golden Globe, SAG Award, and Primetime Emmy, and was nominated for an Academy Award for Ma Rainey's Black Bottom.

Here is a comprehensive biography of **Mary I of England**:

---

## Mary I of England (1516–1558)

**Queen of England and Ireland (1553–1558)**

Mary I was the first queen regnant of England, ruling from July 1553 until her death on 17 November 1558. She is best known for her determined attempt to reverse the English Reformation and restore Roman Catholicism as the state religion — and for the violent persecution of Protestant dissenters that earned her the enduring nickname **"Bloody Mary."**

---

### Early Life and Family

Mary Tudor was born on **18 February 1516** at the **Palace of Placentia** in Greenwich, England. She was the only surviving child of **King Henry VIII** and his first wife, **Catherine of Aragon** (daughter of the famed Spanish monarchs Ferdinand and Isabella). Before Mary's birth, Catherine had endured multiple miscarriages, stillbirths, and one short-lived son, Henry, Duke of Cornwall.

Mary was baptized into the Catholic faith three days after her birth at the Church of the Observant Friars in Greenwich. Her godparents included Lord Chancellor Thomas Wolsey and her great-aunt Catherine, Countess of Devon.

### Childhood and Education

Mary was a **precocious and talented child**. By the age of four and a half, she entertained a visiting French delegation by performing on the virginals (a type of harpsichord). A great deal of her early education came from her mother, Catherine, who commissioned the Spanish humanist **Juan Luis Vives** to write *De Institutione Feminae Christianae*, a treatise on the education of girls. By age nine, Mary could read and write **Latin**, and she also studied French, Spanish, music, dance, and possibly Greek.

Henry VIII doted on his daughter and famously boasted to the Venetian ambassador that "she never cried." Mary had a fair complexion, pale blue eyes, and red or reddish-golden hair — traits resembling both her parents.

Despite his affection, Henry was deeply disappointed that the marriage had produced no sons. In **1525**, Henry sent the nine-year-old Mary to the Welsh border to preside over the **Council of Wales and the Marches**, giving her a court at **Ludlow Castle** and many of the prerogatives normally reserved for a Prince of Wales — though she was never formally invested with that title.

### Marriage Negotiations

Throughout Mary's childhood, Henry negotiated numerous potential marriages for her:

- **Age 2**: Promised to Francis, Dauphin of France (infant son of King Francis I), but the contract was repudiated after three years.
- **Age 6 (1522)**: Contracted to marry her 22-year-old cousin **Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor**, but Charles broke off the engagement within a few years.
- Further negotiations with France and Scotland were discussed but never materialized.

### The King's "Great Matter" and Mary's Downfall

The defining crisis of Mary's youth came when Henry VIII, desperate for a male heir, sought to annul his marriage to Catherine of Aragon. Henry claimed the marriage was invalid because Catherine had previously been married to his deceased brother, Arthur, Prince of Wales. **Pope Clement VII** refused to grant the annulment.

In **1533**, Henry married **Anne Boleyn** and had Archbishop Thomas Cranmer declare his marriage to Catherine void. Henry broke with Rome, declaring himself **Supreme Head of the Church of England**. The consequences for Mary were devastating:

- She was declared **illegitimate** and stripped of the title "Princess."
- She was styled merely "The Lady Mary."
- Her place in the line of succession was transferred to the newborn **Princess Elizabeth** (Henry's daughter with Anne Boleyn).
- Her household was dissolved, her servants dismissed, and she was sent to serve in her infant half-sister's household at Hatfield Palace.

Mary **stubbornly refused** to acknowledge Anne as queen or Elizabeth as a princess, which enraged Henry. The relationship between father and daughter deteriorated so badly that they did not speak for **three years**. Mary was frequently ill during this period, suffering from irregular menstruation and depression. She was denied permission to visit her dying mother. When **Catherine of Aragon died in 1536**, Mary was "inconsolable."

### Reconciliation with Henry

In **1536**, Anne Boleyn fell from favor and was beheaded. Henry married **Jane Seymour**, who urged reconciliation with Mary. Henry insisted that Mary recognize him as head of the Church of England, repudiate papal authority, and accept her own illegitimacy. After much resistance, Mary was bullied into signing a document agreeing to all of Henry's demands.

Reconciled with her father, Mary returned to court. In **1537**, Jane Seymour died after giving birth to a son, **Edward**, who displaced Mary as heir apparent. Mary served as godmother to her half-brother and acted as chief mourner at Jane's funeral.

In **1543**, Henry's sixth wife, **Catherine Parr**, helped bring the family closer together. Through the **Third Succession Act (1544)**, Henry restored Mary and Elizabeth to the line of succession after Edward — though both remained legally illegitimate.

### Accession to the Throne

Henry VIII died in **1547**, and the nine-year-old **Edward VI** succeeded him. A Protestant regency council dominated the government and pushed through sweeping religious reforms. Mary remained steadfastly Catholic, defiantly hearing traditional Mass in her own chapel.

When Edward became terminally ill in **1553**, he desperately tried to exclude Mary from the succession, fearing — correctly — that she would reverse his Protestant reforms. On his deathbed, he named **Lady Jane Grey** (his cousin once removed) as his heir, bypassing both Mary and Elizabeth.

On **6 July 1553**, Edward VI died. Mary was warned that a summons to London was a trap, so she fled to **East Anglia**, where she assembled a military force at **Framlingham Castle**. Support for the Protestant Jane Grey collapsed rapidly. Jane was deposed on **19 July**, and Mary rode triumphantly into London on **3 August 1553** with over 800 nobles and gentlemen, on a wave of popular support. She was crowned at **Westminster Abbey** on **1 October 1553**.

### Reign: The Catholic Restoration

Mary's reign was dominated by her single-minded mission to restore **Roman Catholicism** to England. She:

- Released prominent Catholic prisoners from the Tower of London.
- Repealed the Protestant religious legislation of Edward VI's reign.
- Attempted to restore Church property confiscated during the previous two reigns (largely thwarted by Parliament).
- Reconciled England with **Rome** through Cardinal Reginald Pole.

### The Marian Persecutions ("Bloody Mary")

The most notorious aspect of Mary's reign was the **burning of Protestant "heretics."** During her five-year rule, **more than 280 religious dissenters** were burned at the stake — including bishops, clergy, and ordinary citizens. These persecutions became known as the **Marian Persecutions** and were deeply unpopular, even among some Catholics. They cemented Mary's posthumous reputation as **"Bloody Mary."**

### Marriage to Philip of Spain

At age 37, Mary sought to marry and produce a Catholic heir to prevent Elizabeth from succeeding. Against the wishes of many of her subjects — who feared foreign domination — she married **Philip of Spain** (later **Philip II**) on **25 July 1554**. Philip was the son of Mary's cousin, Emperor Charles V.

The marriage was deeply unpopular. Rebellions broke out, including **Wyatt's Rebellion** (January 1554), which Mary suppressed with determination. The marriage treaty carefully limited Philip's power in England, but the English feared being drawn into Spain's wars.

Mary reportedly fell deeply in love with Philip, but the affection was not reciprocated. In **1554**, she believed she was pregnant, but the anticipated child never arrived — likely a **phantom pregnancy**. The same thing happened again in **1557–1558**. These false pregnancies were deeply humiliating and may have been symptoms of a serious medical condition.

### Death and Legacy

Mary fell ill in **May 1558**, possibly suffering from **ovarian cysts** or **uterine cancer**. She died on **17 November 1558**, aged 42, at **St James's Palace**, during an influenza epidemic. Her trusted advisor, Cardinal Pole, died on the same day.

Philip, who was in Brussels, wrote to his sister: *"I felt a reasonable regret for her death."*

Mary was buried at **Westminster Abbey** on 14 December 1558. She shares a tomb with her half-sister and successor, **Elizabeth I**. The Latin inscription, added by King James I, reads:

> *"Regno consortes et urna, hic obdormimus Elizabetha et Maria sorores, in spe resurrectionis"*
> ("Consorts in realm and tomb, we sisters Elizabeth and Mary here lie down to sleep in hope of the resurrection.")

She was succeeded by **Elizabeth I**, who promptly reversed Mary's Catholic restoration and established the Protestant **Elizabethan Religious Settlement**.

---

### Key Facts at a Glance

| Detail | Information |
|--------|------------|
| **Born** | 18 February 1516, Palace of Placentia, Greenwich |
| **Parents** | Henry VIII and Catherine of Aragon |
| **House** | Tudor |
| **Reign** | July 1553 – 17 November 1558 |
| **Coronation** | 1 October 1553 |
| **Spouse** | Philip II of Spain (m. 25 July 1554) |
| **Religion** | Roman Catholicism |
| **Died** | 17 November 1558, aged 42, St James's Palace |
| **Buried** | Westminster Abbey |
| **Successor** | Elizabeth I |
| **Motto** | *"Veritas Temporis Filia"* ("Truth, the Daughter of Time") |

### Historical Assessment

Mary I's reign, though brief and ultimately unsuccessful in its central aim of restoring Catholicism permanently to England, was historically significant. She was England's first queen regnant, proving that a woman could rule in her own right. However, her legacy was overwhelmingly shaped by the **Marian Persecutions**, her unpopular Spanish marriage, and the failure to produce an heir — leading to the very Protestant succession she had sought to prevent. The contrast with her successor's celebrated "Elizabethan Age" further darkened Mary's historical reputation for centuries.

[Source: Wikipedia — "Mary I of England"]

## Glover Teixeira — Biography

### Early Life
Glover Teixeira was born on **October 28, 1979**, in the small town of **Sobrália, Minas Gerais, Brazil**. He grew up on a farm and knew little about mixed martial arts during his childhood. At age 19, he moved to **Connecticut, United States**, where he would eventually discover and pursue combat sports. He trained under **John Hackleman** at The Pit — the same renowned coach who trained UFC legend Chuck Liddell — and earned a **5th-degree black belt in Kajukenbo** (a Hawaiian hybrid martial art). [Sources: ESPN "My Life as a Fighter" profile; Weibo MMA posts]

### Physical Attributes & Style
- **Height:** 6'2" (188 cm) | **Weight:** 205 lbs (Light Heavyweight)
- **Reach:** 76 inches
- **Stance:** Orthodox
- **Style:** Primarily a striker with elite Brazilian Jiu-Jitsu submission skills

### Professional MMA Career

**Record: 33–9–0** (18 wins by KO/TKO, 10 by submission, 5 by decision)

Teixeira began his professional MMA career in 2002 and compiled an extraordinary **20-fight winning streak over nine years** before even getting his first UFC title shot — one of the longest such streaks in MMA history. [Source: MMA Fighting]

#### UFC Tenure (2012–2023)
- **UFC Debut:** May 26, 2012 — defeated Kyle Kingsbury by submission (rear-naked choke) [Source: Baidu]
- Notable early UFC wins include a **1st-round TKO of Ryan Bader** at UFC Fight Night 28 (September 2013), which earned him a Knockout of the Night bonus [Source: Weibo], and a **unanimous decision over Quinton "Rampage" Jackson** in January 2013 [Source: Baidu].

#### First Title Challenge (2014)
At **UFC 172** (April 26, 2014), Teixeira challenged **Jon Jones** for the UFC Light Heavyweight Championship. He lost by unanimous decision after five hard-fought rounds, but going the distance with the dominant champion was considered an impressive achievement. [Source: MMA Fighting]

#### Mid-Career Struggles & Resurgence
After the Jones loss, Teixeira went **5–4** in his next nine fights, losing to elite competition including Phil Davis, Anthony Johnson, Alexander Gustafsson, and Corey Anderson. Many wrote him off as a "gatekeeper" in the division.

However, he then mounted a remarkable **five-fight win streak** against top competition, including:
- **TKO win over Anthony Smith** (May 2020, UFC Fight Night)
- **Submission win over Thiago Santos** (November 2020, UFC Vegas 13)

During this run, he set **UFC Light Heavyweight division records** for most finishes (12) and most submissions (6) — surpassing all previous light heavyweights in UFC history. [Source: MMA Fighting]

#### UFC Light Heavyweight Champion (2021–2022)
At **UFC 267** (October 30, 2021) in Abu Dhabi, the 42-year-old Teixeira defeated champion **Jan Błachowicz** by rear-naked choke submission in the second round (3:02) to become the **UFC Light Heavyweight Champion**. He became one of the oldest first-time champions in UFC history, a testament to his extraordinary perseverance. [Source: ESPN]

His reign was short-lived. At **UFC 275** (June 11, 2022), he lost the title to **Jiří Procházka** via submission in the fifth round (4:32) in a thrilling, back-and-forth Fight of the Year candidate. [Source: ESPN]

### Retirement
Teixeira's final fight came at **UFC 283** (January 21, 2023) in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil, where he faced **Jamahal Hill** for the vacant light heavyweight title. He lost by unanimous decision. After the fight, the 43-year-old **announced his retirement**, leaving his gloves in the Octagon in the traditional MMA retirement gesture. [Source: ESPN]

### Post-Retirement: Coach & Mentor
Following retirement, Teixeira transitioned into a coaching role and became the **head cornerman and close mentor to Alex Pereira**, the former UFC Middleweight and Light Heavyweight Champion. Their partnership has been widely praised, with Teixeira's experience and tactical acumen playing a key role in Pereira's success at the highest level. [Source: FOX Sports]

### Legacy
Glover Teixeira is remembered as one of the most **perseverant, tough, and respected fighters** in UFC history. His journey — from a small farm in Brazil to becoming a UFC world champion at age 42 — is regarded as one of the most inspirational stories in mixed martial arts. He holds the UFC Light Heavyweight records for most finishes and most submissions, and is celebrated for his humble, soft-spoken demeanor in a sport often dominated by trash talk. [Source: MMA Fighting]

# Biography of Sajid Nadiadwala

**Sajid Nadiadwala** (born 18 February 1966, Mumbai, Maharashtra, India) is a prominent Indian **film producer, director, and screenwriter** working in Hindi cinema. He is the founder and owner of **Nadiadwala Grandson Entertainment** (established 1992) and the grandson of filmmaker **A.K. Nadiadwala**.

---

## Early Life
Sajid's grandfather, Abdul Karim Nadiadwala, migrated to Mumbai from **Nadiad, Gujarat** in 1955 and produced hundreds of films, including *Taj Mahal*. After completing his undergraduate degree, Sajid worked as an assistant director under **J.P. Dutta** on the sets of *Ghulami* at the age of 21. He began his career as a production assistant at his uncle's production house before founding his own company at age 25.

---

## Career Highlights

### Debut & Early Years (1992–1995)
- Produced his first film, **Zulm Ki Hukumat** (1992), starring Dharmendra and Govinda.
- Brought together **Akshay Kumar** and **Suniel Shetty** for the first time in **Waqt Hamara Hai** (1993).

### Rise to Prominence (1996–2006)
- **Jeet** (1996) – Salman Khan and Sunny Deol starrer; a major hit.
- **Judwaa** (1997) – Salman Khan in a double role; became a cult classic.
- **Har Dil Jo Pyar Karega** (2000) and **Mujhse Shaadi Karogi** (2004) – successful multi-starrers.
- **Jaan-E-Mann** (2006) – commercially unsuccessful.

### Major Successes (2007–2014)
- **Heyy Babyy** (2007) – launched director Sajid Khan.
- **Kambakkht Ishq** (2009) – featured Hollywood stars **Sylvester Stallone**, **Denise Richards**, and **Brandon Routh**, a first for Bollywood.
- **Housefull** (2010) – launched one of Bollywood's most successful comedy franchises.
- **Housefull 2** (2012) – entered the ₹100-crore club.
- **2014** was a landmark year with four hits: **Highway** (Alia Bhatt, Randeep Hooda), **2 States** (Alia Bhatt, Arjun Kapoor), **Heropanti** (launched **Tiger Shroff** and **Kriti Sanon**), and **Kick** — his **directorial debut** starring Salman Khan and Jacqueline Fernandez. *Kick* became one of the top 100 highest-grossing Indian films.

### Continued Dominance (2015–2019)
- **Phantom** (2015), **Tamasha** (2015)
- **Baaghi** (2016), **Housefull 3** (2016), **Dishoom** (2016)
- **Judwaa 2** (2017) – earned over ₹223 crores worldwide.
- **Baaghi 2** (2018) – earned over ₹258 crores worldwide.
- **Chhichhore** (2019) – won the **National Film Award for Best Feature Film in Hindi**.
- **Super 30** (2019), **Housefull 4** (2019), **Kalank** (2019)

### Recent Work (2020–Present)
- **Baaghi 3** (2020), **Tadap** (2021), **83** (2021)
- **Bachchhan Paandey** (2022), **Heropanti 2** (2022)
- **Satyaprem Ki Katha** (2023), **Bawaal** (2023)
- **Chandu Champion** (2024)
- **Sikandar** (2025), **Housefull 5** (2025), **Baaghi 4** (2025)
- Upcoming: **O'Romeo** (2026)

---

## Awards
| Year | Award | Film | Result |
|------|-------|------|--------|
| 2015 | IIFA Award for Debut Director | *Kick* | **Won** |
| 2020 | National Film Award for Best Feature Film in Hindi | *Chhichhore* | **Won** |
| 2015 | Filmfare Best Film Award | *2 States* | Nominated |
| 2020 | Filmfare Best Film Award | *Chhichhore* | Nominated |

---

## Personal Life
- **First marriage**: Married actress **Divya Bharti** on 10 May 1992 in a private Nikah ceremony. Tragically, she died on 5 April 1993 after accidentally falling from the balcony of her 5th-floor apartment in Mumbai.
- **Second marriage**: Married journalist **Warda Khan** on 18 November 2000 (Warda proposed to Sajid). They have **two sons**.
- He is closely associated with Bollywood stars **Salman Khan**, **Akshay Kumar**, and **Tiger Shroff**.
- His cousin is **Firoz Nadiadwala**, also a film producer.

---

Sajid Nadiadwala is regarded as one of the most commercially successful producers in Bollywood, known for launching major franchises (*Housefull*, *Baaghi*, *Heropanti*), introducing new talent, and consistently delivering box-office hits across multiple genres over a career spanning more than three decades.

*[Source: Wikipedia – "Sajid Nadiadwala"]*

I have comprehensive information from Wikipedia about Adil Rami. Let me compile a well-structured biography covering his early life, club career, international career, and personal life.# Adil Rami — Biography

## Early Life

**Adil Rami** (Arabic: عَادِل رَامِي) was born on **27 December 1985** in **Bastia, Corsica, France**, to Moroccan parents. He holds both French and Moroccan citizenship. As a youth, his family moved to the mainland, settling in the southern coastal city of **Fréjus**, where his mother worked as a member of the city council. Rami is the third of four children, with two sisters and one brother.

While pursuing his dream of becoming a professional footballer, Rami worked for the city of Fréjus — doing odd jobs such as maintenance and cleaning — to support himself, as his family was concerned about his future prospects.

---

## Club Career

### Fréjus (1994–2006)
Rami registered as a youth player for **Étoile Fréjus Saint-Raphaël** (then ES Fréjus) at age nine in 1994. He initially played as an offensive midfielder, but after an injury to a teammate, his coaches moved him into central defence — a transition that proved highly successful. He played in the **Championnat de France amateur** (fourth division), appearing in **58 matches** across three seasons (2003–2006) without scoring.

### Lille (2006–2011)
In 2006, Rami jumped three divisions to join **Ligue 1** club **Lille OSC**. He made his professional debut on 19 May 2007 against Auxerre and signed his first professional contract (a three-year deal) shortly after. Over time, he established himself as a starter at centre-back, earning the nickname **"Shrek"** from teammates and supporters.

His breakthrough **2008–09 season** saw him score 4 goals in 33 league appearances. He was instrumental in Lille's **2010–11 campaign**, during which the club won the **Ligue 1 title and the Coupe de France** (league and cup double) — though by January 2011 he had already signed with Valencia and was loaned back to Lille for the remainder of the season.

In total, Rami made **163 appearances and scored 10 goals** for Lille.

### Valencia (2011–2014)
On 3 January 2011, **Valencia CF** confirmed Rami's signing for a reported fee of **€6–10 million**. He made his debut in a 4–3 win over Racing de Santander, scoring in the same match. He made **93 appearances with 8 goals** for the Spanish club.

His time at Valencia ended controversially: in September 2013, he was **suspended** after publicly criticizing his coach Miroslav Đukić and teammates in a radio interview, and was fined a record **€275,000**.

### AC Milan (2014–2015)
Rami joined **AC Milan** initially on loan in January 2014, then permanently in July 2014 on a three-year deal for **€4.25 million**. Notably, Rami personally paid **€500,000** out of pocket to cover the difference between Milan's offer and Valencia's asking price. He scored **4 goals in 44 total appearances** for the Rossoneri.

### Sevilla (2015–2017)
In July 2015, Rami moved to **Sevilla FC** for a fee of around **€3.5 million**. He made **49 appearances and scored 3 goals** during his two seasons in Andalusia.

### Marseille (2017–2019)
Rami returned to France, joining **Olympique de Marseille** in July 2017 on a four-year contract for **€6 million**. He played a key role in Marseille's run to the **2018 UEFA Europa League Final** (where they lost 3–0 to Atlético Madrid). He made **75 appearances with 3 goals** for the club.

His Marseille stint ended in controversy: in August 2019, he was **sacked for "gross misconduct"** after missing a training session — claiming injury — while actually participating in the recording of the French TV game show *Fort Boyard*, which involved physically demanding challenges including mud wrestling.

### Fenerbahçe (2019–2020)
Two weeks after his Marseille dismissal, Rami signed a short-term deal with Turkish club **Fenerbahçe**, making just **7 appearances**.

### Sochi (2020)
In February 2020, Rami joined Russian club **PFC Sochi**, but his contract was terminated in May 2020 during the COVID-19 pandemic without him playing a single match. Rami claimed he was never paid.

### Boavista (2020–2021)
Rami moved to Portugal's **Boavista FC**, playing **22 matches** before leaving by mutual consent in July 2021.

### Troyes (2021–2023)
On 24 August 2021, Rami signed with **ES Troyes AC** in Ligue 1. He made **39 appearances and scored 3 goals** over two seasons, effectively concluding his professional playing career.

**Career totals: 523+ club appearances, 31 goals.**

---

## International Career

Despite being courted by **Morocco** on multiple occasions (for the 2008 Africa Cup of Nations and subsequent friendlies), Rami consistently declined, citing his ambition to play for **France**.

- **Debut:** 11 August 2010 in a friendly against Norway, under manager Laurent Blanc.
- **UEFA Euro 2012:** Played every minute of France's four matches (group stage + quarter-final vs. Spain).
- **UEFA Euro 2016:** Called up to replace the injured Raphaël Varane. Started every minute of the first four matches (group stage + round of 16). France finished as **runners-up**, losing 1–0 to Portugal in the final.
- **2018 FIFA World Cup:** Named to the 23-man squad for Russia 2018. France won the tournament, making Rami a **World Cup winner**, though he was the only unused outfield player in the squad. He announced his international retirement after the victory.

**International record: 36 caps, 1 goal.**

### Honors
| Competition | Result |
|---|---|
| **2018 FIFA World Cup (Russia)** | 🏆 **Winner** |
| **UEFA Euro 2016 (France)** | 🥈 Runner-up |

---

## Personal Life

Rami is the father of **twins, Zayn and Madi** (born 2016), with former partner Sidonie Biémont. In 2017, he began a high-profile relationship with actress **Pamamela Anderson**; they lived together in a mansion in Cassis, near Marseille. In June 2019, Anderson accused him of **domestic abuse** in an Instagram post and announced their breakup.

Rami is a **Muslim** of Moroccan descent.

---

## Post-Playing Career

In 2025, Rami transitioned into French television, competing in **season 14 of *Danse avec les stars*** (the French version of *Dancing with the Stars*), where he reached the final and finished **third**. He also appeared in **season 4 of *Les Traîtres*** (The Traitors).

---

*Sources: Wikipedia — "Adil Rami"; FIFA; LFP; Valencia CF; Olympique de Marseille official profiles.*

---

## Tim Fischer (1946–2019)

**Timothy Andrew Fischer AC FTSE** was an Australian politician and diplomat who served as **leader of the National Party from 1990 to 1999** and as Australia's **tenth Deputy Prime Minister** (1996–1999) in the Howard government.

### Early Life & Military Service

Tim Fischer was born on **3 May 1946** in **Lockhart, New South Wales**, the fourth of five children. His parents came from once-wealthy Melbourne families affected by the Great Depression. He grew up on the family property at **Boree Creek, NSW**, attending Boree Creek Public School before boarding at **Xavier College** in Melbourne from 1958.

In **1966**, Fischer was conscripted into the **Australian Army** and served as a **Second Lieutenant** with the **1st Battalion, Royal Australian Regiment (1RAR)** in the **Vietnam War** from July 1966 to March 1969. He was wounded during the **Battle of Coral-Balmoral** in May–June 1968. His battalion was awarded the Unit Citation for Gallantry for those actions.

### Political Career

#### State Politics (1971–1984)
Upon returning from Vietnam, Fischer took over the family farm and became active in the Country Party (later the National Party). He entered the **New South Wales Legislative Assembly** in 1971, representing:
- **Sturt** (1971–1980)
- **Murray** (1980–1984)

He served on the opposition frontbench from 1978 to 1984.

#### Federal Politics (1984–2001)
In 1984, Fischer won the federal seat of **Farrer** in western NSW. He rose through the ranks and, in **1990**, succeeded **Charles Blunt** as **Leader of the National Party**, defeating former leader Ian Sinclair.

Key highlights of his federal career:
- **Deputy Prime Minister** and **Minister for Trade** (1996–1999) under Prime Minister **John Howard**
- Strongly supported **gun control** measures after the 1996 **Port Arthur massacre**, defying much of his rural base
- Fought back against the rise of **Pauline Hanson's One Nation** party, which threatened the Nationals' rural heartland
- Pushed for the extinguishment of native title rights following the **Mabo and Wik decisions**, attracting significant criticism
- Earned the affectionate nickname **"Two-Minute Tim"** for his whirlwind schedule of rural visits

In **1994**, Fischer was seriously injured in a car accident near Lockhart, in which the occupants of the other vehicle were killed. He took a month off for recovery.

He **resigned as party leader in 1999** and retired from politics in **2001**, motivated in part by family demands — his son Harrison has autism. Fischer himself claimed to have "high functioning" autism, though he was never professionally diagnosed.

### Post-Political Career

After politics, Fischer remained extremely active:
- **Chairman of Tourism Australia** (2004–2007)
- **Australia's first resident Ambassador to the Holy See** (2009–2012), nominated by PM Kevin Rudd. He worked closely with the Vatican on the canonisation of **Mary MacKillop**, Australia's first Catholic saint.
- Chairman and patron of the **Crawford Fund** (international agricultural research)
- Vice-chair and chair of the **Crop Trust** (2013–2017) and supporter of the **Svalbard Global Seed Vault**
- National chairman of the **Royal Flying Doctor Service**
- Founding Patron of **Australia for UNHCR** (2001–2006)

He was appointed a **Companion of the Order of Australia (AC)** in the 2005 Australia Day Honours.

### Personal Life & Passions

Fischer married **Judy Brewer** in 1992; they had **two sons**.

He was one of **Australia's best-known rail enthusiasts** — a passionate advocate for rail transport since childhood. After politics, he served as special envoy for the **Adelaide-Darwin railway line**, travelled on the first Ghan passenger train to Darwin in 2004, and hosted the ABC Local Radio podcast *The Great Train Show*. An Aurizon locomotive (V544) was named in his honour.

### Publications

Fischer authored several books, including:
- *Seven Days in East Timor: Ballot and Bullets* (2000)
- *Tim Fischer's Outback Heroes* (2003)
- *Transcontinental Train Odyssey* (2004)
- *Trains Unlimited* (2011)
- *Steam Australia: Locomotives that Galvanised the Nation* (2018)

### Death

In October 2018, Fischer was diagnosed with **acute myeloid leukaemia**, which he attributed to exposure to **Agent Orange** during his Vietnam service. He died on **22 August 2019** at the Albury-Wodonga Cancer Centre, aged **73**. He was given a **state funeral** in Albury on 29 August 2019.

Tim Fischer (1946–2019) was an Australian politician and diplomat who served as Leader of the National Party (1990–1999), Deputy Prime Minister of Australia (1996–1999), Minister for Trade, and later as Australia's first resident Ambassador to the Holy See (2009–2012). A Vietnam War veteran wounded at the Battle of Coral-Balmoral, he was known for his passionate support of gun control after the Port Arthur massacre, his battles against Pauline Hanson's One Nation party, and his lifelong advocacy for rail transport. He was appointed Companion of the Order of Australia in 2005 and died of acute myeloid leukaemia in August 2019, aged 73.

---

# Kang Ji-hwan (강지환)

**Born:** March 20, 1977, in Seoul, South Korea

**Occupation:** Former actor (active 2002–2019)

---

## Early Career (2002–2005)

Kang Ji-hwan, born Jo Tae-gyu, began his acting career in **musical theatre** in 2002, performing in Korean stagings of *The Rocky Horror Show* and *Grease*. Adopting the stage name "Kang Ji-hwan," he transitioned to television with small roles in the sitcom *Nonstop 4* and dramas like *Summer Scent* and *Save the Last Dance for Me* (2003–2004).

His breakthrough came in **2005** with the MBC drama **Be Strong, Geum-soon!**, where he played a fastidious doctor who falls for a widowed hairdresser (Han Hye-jin). The drama was a hit and catapulted him to pan-Asian fame, particularly in Japan, China, and Taiwan.

---

## Rise to Stardom (2005–2008)

After his TV breakthrough, Kang made his **film debut** in the independent film *Host and Guest* (2006), playing a Jehovah's Witness who befriends an unemployed professor. The film toured international film festival circuits.

The year **2008** was a turning point:
- He starred as the titular folk hero in **Hong Gil-dong**, a fusion period dramedy written by the famous Hong sisters, which became a cult hit.
- He co-starred with **So Ji-sub** in **Rough Cut**, a critically acclaimed low-budget film about a gangster who wants to become an actor. Both actors won multiple **Best New Actor** awards for their performances, including at the Blue Dragon Film Awards, Baeksang Arts Awards, and Korean Association of Film Critics Awards.

---

## Commercial Peak (2009–2012)

Kang reunited with Kim Ha-neul in the 2009 action-romantic comedy **My Girlfriend Is an Agent**, which became the **fourth highest-grossing Korean film of the year** with over 4 million tickets sold.

Other notable projects during this period:
- **Coffee House** (2010) – A romantic comedy where he played an eccentric novelist caught in a love triangle.
- **Cafe In** (2010) – A stage musical revival that he both produced and starred in, becoming the **first Korean actor to perform at the Tokyo Globe Theatre**.
- **Lie to Me** (2011) – Co-starring Yoon Eun-hye, playing a hotelier and a civil servant who fake a marriage.
- **Runway Cop** (2012) – A comedy where he famously gained 12 kg and then lost 15 kg within two weeks for his role as an overweight detective going undercover as a fashion model.

---

## Later Career (2013–2019)

- **Incarnation of Money** (2013) – Played a prosecutor with ties to a loan shark.
- **Big Man** (2014) – Portrayed an orphan tricked into believing he's the heir to a wealthy family.
- **Monster** (2016) – Reunited with co-star Sung Yu-ri in a revenge melodrama.
- **Children of a Lesser God** (2018) – His first cable drama, playing a genius detective.
- **Feel Good to Die** (2018) – A workplace comedy.
- **Joseon Survival Period** (2019) – His final drama; he was replaced from episode 11 onward due to legal issues.

---

## Awards

Kang was a decorated actor, winning numerous awards including:

| Year | Award | Work |
|------|-------|------|
| 2005 | MBC Drama Awards – Best New Actor | *Be Strong, Geum-soon!* |
| 2005 | MBC Drama Awards – Excellence Award, Actor | *Be Strong, Geum-soon!* |
| 2007 | KBS Drama Awards – Excellence Award, Actor | *Capital Scandal* |
| 2008 | 44th Baeksang Arts Awards – Most Popular Actor (TV) | *Hong Gil-dong* |
| 2008 | Blue Dragon Film Awards – Best New Actor | *Rough Cut* |
| 2009 | 45th Baeksang Arts Awards – Best New Actor (Film) | *Rough Cut* |
| 2009 | Grand Bell Awards – Best New Actor | *My Girlfriend Is an Agent* |

---

## Contract Disputes

Kang faced significant **contract disputes** throughout his career:
- In **2010**, he was sued by his former agency Jambo Entertainment for breach of contract after moving to S-Plus Entertainment. The Corea Entertainment Management Association (CEMA) suspended his activities for eight months.
- In **2012**, S-Plus sued him when he refused a contract extension. The Seoul Central District Court ultimately **ruled in Kang's favor** in 2013, dismissing the lawsuit and ordering S-Plus to pay him ₩5 million in damages for defamation.

---

## Legal Issues and Retirement (2019)

On **July 9, 2019**, Kang was arrested for allegedly sexually molesting and assaulting two female employees of his agency at his home. He initially stated he didn't remember the events after drinking. On **July 15, 2019**, he admitted to all charges and apologized to the victims.

His agency, **Huayi Brothers**, terminated his contract the following day, and Kang retired from the entertainment industry.

On **December 5, 2019**, the Suwon District Court found him guilty, sentencing him to **2 years and 6 months imprisonment, suspended for 3 years of probation**. He was also ordered to complete 120 hours of community service, 40 hours of sexual offender treatment, and face employment restrictions for three years.

---

## Matthew Jack Leach

**Born:** 22 June 1991, Taunton, Somerset, England  
**Nicknames:** "Leachy", "The Nut"  
**Role:** Bowler — Slow left-arm orthodox spin  
**Batting:** Left-handed  
**Teams:** Somerset (2010–present), England (2018–present)

---

### Early Life & Education

Jack Leach was born and raised in **Taunton**, the home of Somerset County Cricket Club. He attended Trinity School, Bishop Fox's School, and Richard Huish College, all in Taunton. Before his cricket career took off, he worked collecting trolleys at a Sainsbury's supermarket in Taunton. At the age of **14**, he was diagnosed with **Crohn's disease**, a chronic inflammatory bowel condition that he has managed throughout his professional career. He later studied sports coaching at the University of Wales Institute, Cardiff (Cardiff Metropolitan University). [来源: Wikipedia — Jack Leach]

---

### Domestic Career

Leach graduated from the **Somerset County Cricket Club academy** in the summer of **2010** and signed a professional contract. He first represented **Dorset County Cricket Club** in the 2010 Minor Counties Cricket Championship, bowling them to victory in the final against Lincolnshire with figures of 6/21 in the second innings.

While at university, he played for **Cardiff MCCU** in 2011 and 2012, making his first-class debut against Somerset in March 2012. He made his **Somerset debut** in a two-day match against the touring South Africans in July 2012, notably claiming the wicket of **Hashim Amla**. His first-class debut for Somerset came in August 2012 against Lancashire in the County Championship.

He spent the 2012–13 winter playing grade cricket for **Valley District Cricket Club** in Brisbane, Australia, winning the T20 competition. In the 2013 season, he took his maiden five-wicket haul for Somerset against Warwickshire.

In **April 2022**, he was bought by **Birmingham Phoenix** for The Hundred. He signed a new two-year contract with Somerset in **June 2024**. [来源: Wikipedia — Jack Leach]

---

### International Career

#### Debut & Early Years (2018–2021)

Leach was called up to England's Test squad for the **2018 tour of New Zealand** as an injury replacement for Mason Crane. He made his **Test debut** in the second Test in **Christchurch** on 30 March 2018.

He gained national fame during the **2019 Ashes** series. In the one-off Test against Ireland at Lord's earlier that summer, he scored a remarkable **92 runs** as a nightwatchman, earning Man of the Match. But his most iconic moment came in the **third Ashes Test at Headingley** (August 2019): he scored a single run (1 not out) in a dramatic unbroken 76-run partnership with **Ben Stokes**, helping England win by one wicket in what has been called "arguably the greatest one not out in the history of the game." His glasses-wearing, methodical batting style and his habit of cleaning his spectacles during play earned him a **cult following** — and free Specsavers glasses for life.

He played a pivotal role in the final Ashes Test that summer, bowling England to victory with figures of **4/49**. However, opportunities remained sporadic: he missed tours due to illness and was often overlooked in favour of Moeen Ali or Dom Bess. [来源: Wikipedia — Jack Leach]

#### First-Choice Spinner (2021–present)

Leach's fortunes changed under the captaincy of **Ben Stokes**. After the 2021–22 Ashes (a difficult tour where he endured a tough first Test at The Gabba), he became Stokes's trusted spinner. Key highlights include:

- **2022:** Took 10–166 in a match against New Zealand — his first ten-wicket haul in Tests. Ended the year as the **third-highest wicket-taker in the world** in Test cricket, behind only Kagiso Rabada and Nathan Lyon.
- **2022–23 Winter:** Took 25 wickets across five Tests against Pakistan and New Zealand, including a five-wicket haul in Wellington.
- **2023 Summer:** Ruled out of the Ashes with a **lower back stress fracture** after the first Test.
- **January 2024:** Suffered a **knee injury** in the first Test against India and missed the remainder of the series.
- **October 2024:** Made a successful return to the England team for the **Pakistan tour**, taking seven wickets in the first Test including 4/30 in the second innings, leading England to a record-breaking victory.

Stokes's handling of Leach has been widely praised — notably keeping attacking fields even when batters looked to go after him. [来源: Wikipedia — Jack Leach]

---

[来源: ESPNcricinfo, via Wikipedia]

---

### Summary

Jack Leach's journey is one of **perseverance and resilience**. From stacking supermarket trolleys in Taunton to becoming England's frontline Test spinner, he has overcome Crohn's disease, years of being in and out of the team, and multiple injuries. His partnership with Ben Stokes at Headingley in 2019 cemented his place in English cricket folklore, and under Stokes's captaincy, he blossomed into one of the world's leading Test spinners. He remains a beloved figure in the game, known for his understated determination and his signature glasses.

---

## 1. Gerhard Fischer — Inventor of the Portable Metal Detector (1899–1988)

Gerhard Julius Richard Fischer (later anglicized to "Fisher") was born on March 18, 1899, in Landeshut, Silesia (then Germany), to engineer Richard Hugo Georg Arthur Fischer and Margaretha Julia Fischer née Job. Around 1906, the family moved to Dresden, where he later studied electrical engineering at Dresden University.

He emigrated to the United States, where he worked with aircraft radio direction finders. He noticed that environmental changes — particularly the presence of metal — caused interference in his radio-direction-finding equipment. He realized this principle could be used to detect metal objects, and even discussed the idea with **Albert Einstein**, who correctly predicted the widespread use of handheld metal detectors. In **1931**, Fisher founded **Fisher Research Laboratory, Inc.** in Palo Alto, California, and in **1937** he patented his portable metal detector. He served as president of the company until his retirement in 1967. He died in 1988 in Palo Alto. [Source: Wikipedia — Gerhard Fischer (inventor)]

---

## 2. Gerhard Fischer — German Diplomat & Humanitarian (1921–2006)

Gerhard Fischer was born on September 20, 1921, in **Oslo, Norway**, the elder son of a Norwegian mother and a German sinologist father, Martin Fischer. At around age 3, the family moved to **China**, where his father worked. He began studying medicine at **Beijing Medical University** and volunteered with leprosy patients — an experience that shaped his entire life's mission.

His medical studies were cut short by the Japanese occupation. He traveled via Siberia to Germany, but was instead conscripted into **World War II**, fighting primarily on the Eastern Front. He spent 2 years and 9 months as a **prisoner of war** in France. Destitute after the war, he worked as a truck driver and other jobs, eventually earning a **law degree** in just one year (a course that normally took four). He joined the German foreign service in 1952.

### Diplomatic Career
Fischer served in **Addis Ababa**, **Hong Kong**, and as Consul in **Madras** (now Chennai), India, where he helped establish the **Indian Institute of Technology Madras** with German government support. He became Ambassador to **Malaysia** (1970–74), **Ireland** (1977–80), the **Netherlands** (1980–83), and **Switzerland** (1983–85). In December 1985, just six months before normal retirement, he **resigned** to devote himself full-time to leprosy and polio patients in India.

### Humanitarian Work
Fischer's dedication to leprosy patients began in Beijing and continued throughout his diplomatic postings. After retiring, he spent six months each year in India, establishing health centres, workshops, schools, wells, and latrines across the country. He emphasized **rehabilitation** over mere treatment — training patients in skills like weaving and carpet-making so they could earn a living. He famously refused to wear gloves or masks, touching every patient and saying *"Nalla thane irukku"* ("You are all right" in Tamil). He insisted patients never be called "lepers" but "leprosy patients."

### Gandhi Peace Prize
In **1997**, Fischer received the **Gandhi Peace Prize** from the President of India, K. R. Narayanan. The selection committee was unanimous. He used the 10 million rupee award to establish a foundation for his humanitarian work.

He died on **July 3, 2006**, in Copenhagen, Denmark, aged 84. His daughter Karen Fischer Koch continued his work. [Source: Wikipedia — Gerhard Fischer (diplomat)]

---

## 3. Gerhard Fischer — Computer Science Professor (born 1945)

Gerhard Fischer was born on **July 2, 1945**. He is **Professor Emeritus of Computer Science**, a Fellow of the Institute of Cognitive Science, and the founder and director of the **Center for LifeLong Learning & Design (L3D)** at the **University of Colorado, Boulder**.

### Education
He earned a Master's in Mathematics and Physical Education from **Heidelberg University** (1971), spent two years at the University of British Columbia and UC Irvine on a DAAD fellowship, and obtained a **PhD in Computer Science** from the **University of Hamburg** (1977). He held postdoctoral positions at **MIT** (working with Seymour Papert and the LOGO community) and **Xerox PARC** (working with Alan Kay and the Smalltalk community).

### Career & Research
From 1978 to 1984, he was Assistant and Associate Professor at the **University of Stuttgart**, with extended visits to **Carnegie Mellon University** to work with **Herbert A. Simon** (Nobel laureate), who served as his Habilitation advisor. In 1984, he joined the University of Colorado Boulder.

His research spans **human-computer interaction, meta-design, social creativity, cultures of participation, lifelong learning, intelligence augmentation**, and **human-centered AI**. He served as principal advisor to 20 PhD students and participated in developing numerous NSF research programs.

### Awards
- **SIGCHI Academy** (2007) — for combining European and American research traditions
- **ACM Fellow** (2009) — for contributions to HCI and computer-mediated lifelong learning
- **RIGO Award** from ACM SIGDOC (2012)
- **EUSSET-IISI Lifetime Achievement Award** (2025)
- **Honorary Doctorate**, University of Gothenburg, Sweden (2015)

He retired as Professor Emeritus in 2012 but remains active in research on design trade-offs, human-centered AI, and quality of life in the digital age. [Source: Wikipedia — Gerhard Fischer (professor)]

---

## 4. Gerhard Fischer — Norwegian Architect & Archaeologist (1890–1977)

Johan Adolf Gerhard Fischer was born on **July 12, 1890**, in **Bergen, Norway**, the son of architect Adolph Fischer. He studied at the Bergen Technical School, the Norwegian National Academy of Craft and Art Industry in Oslo, and the **Royal Danish Academy of Fine Arts** in Copenhagen.

From 1916 to 1926, he worked for the **Norwegian State Railways** architectural office, designing stations including **Lillestrøm, Notodden, Åneby, Ljan, and Bekkelaget**. From 1938 to 1960, he was a conservator at the **Universitetets Oldsaksamling** (now part of the Museum of Cultural History in Oslo), leading excavations of medieval sites including **Sverresborg** in Trondheim, **Bergenhus Fortress**, **Utstein Abbey**, **Hovedøya Abbey**, **Stavanger Cathedral**, and **Nidarosdomen**.

He authored several important books including *Oslo under Eikaberg* (1950), *Norske kongeborger* (two volumes, 1951 and posthumously 1980), *Domkirken i Stavanger* (1964), and *Domkirken i Trondheim* (1965). He received the **Medal of St. Hallvard** (1956), was decorated **Commander of the Order of St. Olav** (1965), and was an honorary member of the **Society of Antiquaries of Scotland** and the **Society of Antiquaries of London**. He died on September 10, 1977, in Oslo. [Source: Wikipedia — Gerhard Fischer (architect)]

**Eric Nathaniel Hacker** (born c. 2001, in Columbus, Ohio) is an American **para-athlete** who competes in middle- and long-distance running in the **T38 classification** (for athletes with cerebral palsy).

## William Waldegrave, Baron Waldegrave of North Hill — Biography

**William Arthur Waldegrave, Baron Waldegrave of North Hill, PC** (born 15 August 1946) is a British Conservative Party politician who served as a Cabinet minister from 1990 to 1997, and has been a life peer in the House of Lords since 1999. He served as Provost of Eton College from 2009 to 2024 and was Chancellor of the University of Reading from 2016 to 2022.

---

### Early Life & Family
Born in London, Waldegrave is the youngest of seven children of **Geoffrey Waldegrave, 12th Earl Waldegrave** and **Mary Hermione Grenfell**. He bore the courtesy title "The Honourable" from birth as a younger son of an Earl. His elder brother is the 13th Earl Waldegrave. His sister is **Lady Susan Hussey**, who served as a Woman of the Bedchamber to Queen Elizabeth II. The family traces its lineage back to James II through the 1st Earl Waldegrave, a diplomat and ambassador.

### Education
- **Eton College** — won the prestigious Newcastle Scholarship in 1965.
- **Corpus Christi College, Oxford** — served as president of both the Oxford Union and the Oxford University Conservative Association.
- **Harvard University** — attended on a Kennedy Scholarship.
- **All Souls College, Oxford** — elected Prize Fellow in 1971; appointed Distinguished Fellow in 2001 for contributions to politics and international relations.

### Early Career
In 1971, Waldegrave joined the **Central Policy Review Staff (CPRS)**, the government's "Think-Tank," where he was one of the most active members and served as a link between the CPRS and the Conservative Party. He left in December 1973.

### Parliamentary Career (1979–1997)
Elected **MP for Bristol West** in 1979, Waldegrave was regarded as a member of the moderate ("wet") tendency of the Conservative Party, yet rose steadily through the ranks:

**Junior Ministerial Posts (1981–1990):**
- Parliamentary Under-Secretary for Education and Science (1981–1983)
- Parliamentary Under-Secretary for Environment (1983–1985)
- Minister of State for Local Government (1985–1986)
- Minister of State for Environment, Countryside and Planning (1986–1987)
- Minister of State for Housing and Planning (1987–1988)
- Minister of State for Foreign and Commonwealth Affairs (1988–1990) — involved in the arms-to-Iraq policy controversy; exonerated of "duplicitous intent" by the Scott Report

**Cabinet Posts (1990–1997):**
- **Secretary of State for Health** (1990–1992) — appointed just days before Thatcher's resignation
- **Chancellor of the Duchy of Lancaster** (1992–1994) — responsible for public services and science
- **Minister of Agriculture, Fisheries and Food** (1994–1995)
- **Chief Secretary to the Treasury** (1995–1997)

He lost his Commons seat in the 1997 general election to Valerie Davey.

### House of Lords
Created a **life peer** as Baron Waldegrave of North Hill, of Chewton Mendip in Somerset, on 28 July 1999.

### Notable Achievements & Roles
- **Provost of Eton College** (2009–2024)
- **Chancellor of the University of Reading** (2016–2022)
- **Chairman of the Rhodes Trust** (2002–2011); helped create the Mandela Rhodes Foundation
- **Chairman of Trustees, National Museum of Science and Industry** (2002–2010)
- In 1993, as Science Minister, he famously **offered a prize for the best lay explanation of the Higgs boson**, which produced David Miller's celebrated metaphor using Conservative party workers — still one of the most quoted explanations of the Higgs boson

### Private Sector
- Director of Coutts & Company (since 2012)
- Director of Adam & Company (2017–2018)
- Non-executive director of GW Pharmaceuticals

### Personal Life
Married in 1975 to **Caroline Burrows**, a cookery writer and managing director of Leith's School of Food and Wine. They have four children: Katherine, Elizabeth, James, and Harriet.

### Publications
- *The Binding of Leviathan – Conservatism and the Future* (1978)
- *A Different Kind of Weather – A Memoir* (2015) — discusses his political career, the poll tax (which he claims was "all my own work"), and his experiences in the Heath, Thatcher, and Major governments.

**Motto:** *Coelum Non Animum* ("Always The Same Person")

## Julia Faye — Biography

**Julia Faye Maloney** (September 24, 1892 – April 6, 1966), known professionally as **Julia Faye**, was an American actress of both silent and sound films. Over a career spanning more than four decades (1915–1957), she appeared in over 100 films and was closely associated with legendary director Cecil B. DeMille, appearing in more than 30 of his productions. Her roles ranged widely — from maids and ingénues to vamps and queens.

---

### Early Life

Faye was born near Richmond, Virginia. Her father, Robert J. Maloney, worked for the Atchison, Topeka and Santa Fe Railway and died before 1901. Her widowed mother, Emma Louise Elliott, then married Cyrus Demetrios Covell, and Julia took her stepfather's surname. She was raised in St. Louis, Missouri, and attended public and private schools there. She studied for a year at Illinois State University but dreamed of becoming an actress.

In 1915, while visiting friends in Hollywood, she toured a film studio and met actor-director **Christy Cabanne**, who discovered they had been neighbors in St. Louis. Cabanne persuaded Faye's reluctant mother to let her enter motion pictures.

---

### Career

#### Early Roles (1915–1916)
Faye made her debut with bit roles in *Martyrs of the Alamo* and *The Lamb* (both 1915), directed by Cabanne for Triangle Film Corporation. Her first credited role was as Dorothea opposite DeWolf Hopper in *Don Quixote* (1915). She gained popularity in Keystone comedies and even worked for D.W. Griffith in *Intolerance* (1916).

#### Cecil B. DeMille and Paramount (1917–1925)
Her first film for DeMille was *The Woman God Forgot* (1917). She became a regular in DeMille's productions, playing Gloria Swanson's maid in *Male and Female* (1919) and the **Wife of Pharaoh** in the prologue of *The Ten Commandments* (1923) — one of her most famous roles. She was listed among the Paramount Stock Company School alongside Rudolph Valentino, Gloria Swanson, and Wallace Reid.

#### Stardom at DeMille Pictures (1925–1927)
When DeMille left Famous Players–Lasky to form his own company, Faye went with him. *The Volga Boatman* (1926) established her as a leading lady, with her portrayal of a "tiger woman" acclaimed as the most captivating performance of her career to that point. She played **Martha** in *The King of Kings* (1927) and won critical acclaim for the comedy *Turkish Delight* (1927). She was also the **first actress to portray Velma on-screen** in the 1927 film adaptation of *Chicago*.

#### Sound Films (1928–1957)
Faye made her talkie debut in DeMille's first sound film, *Dynamite* (1929). After a brief retirement in the early 1930s, she returned and appeared in nearly every DeMille film from *Union Pacific* (1939) onward. By 1948, her role in *Chicago Deadline* marked her **250th film assignment**. Later notable roles included **Hisham** (Delilah's maidservant) in *Samson and Delilah* (1949), a wardrobe mistress in *The Greatest Show on Earth* (1952), and **Elisheba** (Aaron's wife) in *The Ten Commandments* (1956). Her final role was a dowager in *The Buccaneer* (1958).

---

### Relationship with Cecil B. DeMille

Faye met DeMille in 1917 and became one of his long-time mistresses. DeMille told her she would never become a star because she lacked "the right kind of personality," describing her as the "cute type" or "soubrette." Nevertheless, he kept her in his films for decades. In his 1959 autobiography, DeMille wrote admiringly: *"Julia Faye is truly one of those to whom the calendar is absolutely meaningless... Calendars become obsolete. Julia doesn't."*

---

### Personal Life

- **First marriage**: Harold Leroy Wallick (August 2, 1913), who died in 1918.
- **Second marriage**: Playwright Walter Anthony Merrill (October 24, 1935), with DeMille giving her away at the ceremony. They separated after just ten days and divorced in April 1936, citing incompatibility.
- She lived in Los Feliz, Los Angeles, owning a Colonial Revival mansion on Observatory Avenue.
- In the mid-1940s, she began writing a memoir titled *Flicker Faces*, which remains unpublished. Excerpts have been cited in DeMille biographies.

---

### Death and Legacy

Faye died of **cancer** on April 6, 1966, at her home in Pacific Palisades, Los Angeles, aged 73. Her cremated remains rest at the **Hollywood Forever Cemetery**. She was awarded a **star on the Hollywood Walk of Fame** at 6500 Hollywood Boulevard for her contributions to the American film industry. Her unpublished memoir is preserved in the Cecil B. DeMille Archives at Brigham Young University.

[Source: Wikipedia — "Julia Faye"]
